coredata.dc:title: Innovative biomass-derived P, N-CQDs for the sensitive determination of the novel antiviral baloxavir marboxil in human plasma and dosage forms

coredata.dc:description: This work presents a novel, quick, and economical one-pot synthesis of plant-based phosphorus and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (P, N-CQDs). Microwave treatment of Tricolor Crassula (Crassula ovata) leaves as carbon sources with urea and monobasic ammonium phosphate (NH4H2PO4) as nitrogen and phosphorus sources for 80 s resulted in the formation of blue emissive P, N-CQDs (λex/λem of 320/405 nm). The synthesized CQDs were fully characterized, revealing a great quantum yield (35.23 %) and high phosphorus and nitrogen content. Furthermore, they exhibited remarkable efficiency as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the sensitive determination of a new anti-influenza drug, baloxavir marboxil (BXM), which was recently reported to have wide utility as a medication for COVID-19. BXM quantitatively quenched the native fluorescence of P, N-CQDs through a synergistic combination of inner filter effect and static quenching over the concentration range of 0.2–18.0 μg/mL with a LOD of 0.04 μg/mL. The selectivity of the probe was verified in the presence of different metal ions, co-administered drugs, and potential co-existing excipients. The proposed method was further applied for BXM determination in pharmaceutical tablets and spiked human plasma with good percentage recoveries (100.44 ± 1.02 % and 100.23 ± 1.06 %). Finally, the suggested probe’s greenness was assessed using different metrics, confirming its excellent greenness. The rapid synthesis of P, N-CQDs utilizing inexpensive, low-energy microwave radiation and the lack of organic solvents make the produced probe environmentally friendly. This study expands the applications of P, N-CQDs in biomedical and pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents a novel, quick, and economical one-pot synthesis of plant-based phosphorus and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (P, N-CQDs). Microwave treatment of Tricolor Crassula (Crassula ovata) leaves as carbon sources with urea and monobasic ammonium phosphate (NH4H2PO4) as nitrogen and phosphorus sources for 80 s resulted in the formation of blue emissive P, N-CQDs (λex/λem of 320/405 nm). The synthesized CQDs were fully characterized, revealing a great quantum yield (35.23 %) and high phosphorus and nitrogen content. Furthermore, they exhibited remarkable efficiency as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the sensitive determination of a new anti-influenza drug, baloxavir marboxil (BXM), which was recently reported to have wide utility as a medication for COVID-19. BXM quantitatively quenched the native fluorescence of P, N-CQDs through a synergistic combination of inner filter effect and static quenching over the concentration range of 0.2–18.0 μg/mL with a LOD of 0.04 μg/mL. The selectivity of the probe was verified in the presence of different metal ions, co-administered drugs, and potential co-existing excipients. The proposed method was further applied for BXM determination in pharmaceutical tablets and spiked human plasma with good percentage recoveries (100.44 ± 1.02 % and 100.23 ± 1.06 %). Finally, the suggested probe’s greenness was assessed using different metrics, confirming its excellent greenness. The rapid synthesis of P, N-CQDs utilizing inexpensive, low-energy microwave radiation and the lack of organic solvents make the produced probe environmentally friendly. This study expands the applications of P, N-CQDs in biomedical and pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The global spread of viral diseases has resulted in serious health problems, including physical and psychological harm to humans. Some viruses, such as the influenza virus and coronavirus disease-19 (COVID-19), spread rapidly, reaching almost every country in the world [1]. Because of the high incidence and complications of infected individuals, the development and research of new antiviral drugs has become an urgent need of the time. Flu is one of the most common viral diseases that can pose a major risk to the elderly, disabled, and young people. It should be emphasized that if flu epidemics are not properly controlled, they could harm public health. A novel oral endonuclease inhibitor antiviral drug called baloxavir marboxil (BMX), approved by the FDA in 2018, has demonstrated potential in treating influenza A and B and the management of moderate COVID-19 [2]. It is the first influenza antiviral with a novel mechanism of action licensed in nearly two decades. BMX is a prodrug that is hydrolyzed to the active form, baloxavir acid. BMX suppresses the endonuclease activity of the polymerase acidic (PA) protein, a part of viral RNA polymerase. This endonuclease is a virus-specific enzyme that is essential for viral gene transcription [3]. The main advantage of BMX is that it only requires one dose, which should improve compliance. Its effectiveness in stopping the spread of viruses is enhanced by its capacity to lower viral loads within 24 h of treatment. It's possible that a quicker viral load reduction could improve the clinical outcome by reducing the ensuing lung tissue damage and inflammation.

The determination of BMX was accomplished using a limited number of analytical techniques, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], spectrofluorimetry [5,6], and liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) [7–9]. Some of these techniques are time-consuming, which restricts their broad applicability, while others use a lot of organic solvents, require skilled operators, or necessitate chemical derivatization. Considering these drawbacks, fluorimetric analysis demonstrated numerous noteworthy benefits, including reduced cost, increased sensitivity, and simpler operation in less time [10–18].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent a novel form of carbon-based zero-dimensional material that has drawn a lot of interest because of their exceptional water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, high photostability, exceptional analyte sensitivity and selectivity, and significant Stokes' shifts [19–24]. Although CQDs have numerous unique properties, their low quantum yields (QY) prove to be a drawback. Recently, doping CQDs with heteroatoms, including nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), phosphorus (P), and boron (B), greatly increases the QY of CQDs [25–30]. Through heteroatoms co-doping, their complementary actions can result in distinct electronic configurations [31,32]. N doping produces novel electronic configurations and a higher fluorescence QY because of the identical atomic radii of C and N atoms [33–35]. The P atom has a larger radius than the C atom, allowing it to replace defects and function as an n-type donor, altering the electrochemical characteristics of CQDs [36]. Thus, co-doping with N or P atoms can enhance the photoelectric characteristics and quantum yield (QY) of CQDs. Several methods, such as chemical oxidation, laser ablation, electrochemical carbonization, and hydro/solvothermal treatment, can be utilized for the synthesis of CQDs. Extreme circumstances and time requirements are considered to be the disadvantages of these preparatory approaches [26,37]. In contrast, the microwave synthesis approach offers simplicity, a quick reaction time, uniform particle size, and low energy use [38,39]. Moreover, different green plant sources have been utilized for the synthesis of CQDs, such as long pepper, orange fruit, cantaloupe fruit, and citrus plants. Excessive efforts have been made to find other green agents for the synthesis of CQDs [40–44].

While carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been used in several studies to detect metal ions due to their excellent photoluminescence characteristics, their applications in pharmaceutical analysis are still rather limited. The potential of CQDs as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and specific detection of BXM is investigated in this work, broadening the range of uses for CQDs beyond metal ion sensing. In the current work, a novel and eco-friendly microwave-assisted synthesis of P, N-CQDs was developed for fluorimetric analysis of BXM. For the first time, the P, N-CQDs were produced in a very short time (80 s) through one-step microwave heating of Crassula ovata (Tricolor Crassula) leaves as a green carbon source with urea and ammonium phosphate monobasic (NH4H2PO4) as nitrogen and phosphorus sources. Different techniques, including FT-IR, Field emission TEM, zeta potential, XRD, EDX, UV–Vis spectrophotometry, and spectrofluorimetry, were used to characterize the produced P, N-CQDs, guaranteeing multi-doping of the CQDs. Based on the static quenching and inner filter effect mechanisms, the fluorescence emission of the produced P, N-CQDs was quantitatively decreased by adding increasing concentrations of BXM. This led to the application of the synthesized P, N-CQDs as a fluorescence nanoprobe to measure BXM in spiked human plasma and pharmaceutical tablets. Furthermore, a systematic investigation of the recommended approach's greenness was carried out, and the results revealed that the developed method had the least environmental effect. The novel methodology displayed good selectivity and precision for the BXM determination in pharmaceutical formulations. This method is fast and sensitive, and the generated P, N-CQDs had a significantly high quantum yield.

Ammonium phosphate monobasic (NH4H2PO4, Alpha-Chem, Mumbai, India), urea (Sigma–Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) were used. The Crassula ovate (Tricolor Crassula) leaves were collected from Mansoura University, Egypt. Baloxavir marboxil (99.8 %) was generously donated by Al-Andalus Pharmaceuticals, 6th October City, Egypt. Xofluza® tablets (Lot No. # 3388332; produced by Shionogi Pharma, Tokyo, Japan) containing 20 mg BXM were obtained from a local drugstore. Deionized water, ethanol, acetonitrile, and methanol were obtained. Britton–Robinson buffer (BRB, 0.04 M, pH 2.0–12.0) was formed by mixing acetic acid glacial ACS (99.7 %, density = 1.05 g/mL; Ostim, Ankara, Turkey), orthophosphoric acid (85 %, density = 1.88 g/mL; Ostim, Ankara, Turkey), boric acid glacial ACS, A.R. (99.5 %, density = 1.435 g/mL; Ostim, Ankara, Turkey), and sodium hydroxide.

A Cary Eclipse- Agilent Technologies Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Santa Clara, United States) was used for the fluorescence spectra measurement. UV spectra were measured on a UV/VIS spectrophotometer (PG Instrument, UK). The synthesized P, N-CQDs were characterized using a Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) Jasco 6800 from Tokyo, Japan, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) from Tokyo, Japan. The Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer (Kratos Analytical, Inc., NY) was used to measure X-ray diffraction (XRD). Additionally, energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy coupled to a scanning electron microscope (JEOL, JSM-IT100LA, Japan) was utilized to analyze the elements of P, N-CQDs. A digital analytical balance (Sartorius AG, Germany) was utilized for weighing all materials. A Jenway pH meter (3510) was utilized for all pH measurements. Alpha 1–2 LD plus, Germany, was used for lyophilization. A cooling centrifuge (2–16 KL, Germany) and an ultrasonic bath were also used. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were conducted on a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-ALPHA XPS apparatus (USA) using monochromatic X-ray AL K-alpha radiation −10 to 1350 eV spot size 400 µm at pressure 10-9 mbar with full spectrum pass energy 200 eV and at narrow spectrum 50 eV.

Fresh Tricolor Crassula (Crassula ovata) leaves were obtained from the Faculty of Agriculture, Mansoura University, washed with tap water, then distilled water. The leaves (2.0 g) were cut into small pieces. In a 250-mL baker, urea (2.0 g) and ammonium phosphate monobasic (1.0 g) were mixed with the leaves and heated in the microwave oven for 80 s to produce a charred residue. After cooling, the residue was dissolved in 100 mL deionized water and sonicated for 10 min. The mixture was then purified by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered utilizing a 0.22 µm syringe membrane filter. The P, N-CQDs stock solution was covered in Aluminium foil before being kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

To calculate the QY of P, N-CQDs, two volumetric flasks (each is 5.0 mL) were precisely filled with each aqueous standard solution of quinine sulfate (indicator solution) and P, N-CQDs (test solution). The values of average absorbance for each solution were calculated after three absorbance scans were performed for the indicator and the test solutions. The fluorescence spectra of the test and indicator solutions were determined three times at λex of 320 nm. The integration peak values were computed using the adjusted fluorescence spectra. The QY was then calculated by the following Eq. (1).
(1)

Where I, η, and A represent the integrated fluorescence, refractive index, and absorbance, respectively. The standard used was quinine sulfate (QY is 54.6 % in 0.1 M sulfuric acid). To prevent the fluorescence re-absorption effect, the A of quinine sulfate and PN-CDs solutions were less than 0.05 at λex.

The solid P, N-CQDs were created by freeze-drying a P, N-CQDs solution for three days at −65 °C under vacuum. According to the following Eq. (2), the production yield (PY) was measured after weighing the produced solid mass of P, N-CQDs.
(2)

(MP, N-CQDs is the P, N-CQDs' solid mass, and mS is the starting materials' total mass).

The BXM stock solution (1.0 mg/mL) was freshly prepared by dissolving 0.01 g in 10.0 mL of methanol. To prepare a 100.0 μg/mL BXM working standard solution, 5.0 mL of the stock solution was taken and completed to 50.0 mL with deionized water in a volumetric flask. To determine the optimum conditions for BXM sensing, 50.0 µL of P, N-CQDs working solution and 500.0 μL aliquot of BXM standard solution (100.0 μg/mL) were mixed and completed to 5.0 mL deionized water in a volumetric flask. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0-F) was measured at λex/em of 320/405 nm (F0 and F stand for the fluorescence responses of P, N-CQDs without and with BXM). After optimizing the conditions, various volumes of BXM standard solution in the range 0.2–18.0 μg/mL were mixed with 50.0 μL of P, N-CQDs and 1.0 mL of Britton–Robinson buffer (pH = 6). The mixture was then completed to a volume of 5 mL using deionized water. The same procedure was used for a blank experiment. The F0-F values were measured at λex/em of 320/405 nm and plotted against BXM final concentrations (µg/mL) to create the calibration curve. Then, the regression equation was calculated.

Five Xofluza® tablets were weighed and ground into a fine powder, which was then added to a small flask in an amount equal to 10 mg of BXM. The flask was filled with methanol (50 mL), and it was sonicated for 10 min. After that, the flask's contents were filtered into a 100 mL volumetric flask and completed to the mark with methanol. After transferring increasing volumes of the filtrate to 5 mL volumetric flasks, the steps outlined in the section under “General analytical procedure” were carried out. Using the associated regression equation, the tablets' notional contents were ascertained the values of % recovery were computed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of BXM in spiked human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: Human plasma aliquots of 1.0 mL were vortexed for 1 min after being spiked with different volumes of BXM stock solution (100.0 µg/mL) in centrifuge tubes. Plasma proteins were precipitated with up to 5.0 mL of acetonitrile. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 20 min, the tubes were vortexed for 1 min. Then, 50.0 μL of P, N-CQDs aliquots, and 1.0 mL of Britton–Robinson buffer (pH = 6) were moved to a 5-mL volumetric flask. A volume of 1.0 mL of the supernatant was filtered using a 0.45 μm syringe was added and completed to 5.0 mL with deionized water. The ultimate concentrations were 0.2, 0.6, and 1.0 μg/mL. The process outlined in Section 2.4.1 was followed for all analyses and a blank experiment.

The water-soluble P, N-CQDs were prepared in a very short time (only 80 s) using a green and ultrafast single-step microwave-assisted method. The highly luminescent P, N-CQDs were synthesized utilizing commonly available starting materials and a simple process, with Crassula ovata (Tricolor Crassula) leaves as a good source of carbon, urea as a source of nitrogen, and ammonium phosphate monobasic as phosphorus and nitrogen source. Crassula ovata is an important plant of the Crassulaceae family, which spread over most of southern Africa. It is distinguished by its pink-to-white blooms and green leaves that occasionally have a reddish color around the edges when exposed to sunlight. The leaves are used in Africa to treat corns, epilepsy, and diarrhea by boiling them in milk. Leaf juice is commonly utilized to treat diabetes in India. The Crassula ovata plant has been shown to possess antidiabetic, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. Methanol and aqueous extracts of the leaves and stems exhibit anti-E.coli activity. Furthermore, the aqueous extract demonstrated strong antioxidant activity through the use of H2O2 scavenging and the 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radical techniques. However, both aqueous and methanolic extracts demonstrated a notable antidiabetic effect by inhibiting α-amylase. According to the plant's phytochemical analysis, it contained flavonoids, phenols, alkaloids, sterols, carbohydrates, and saponins [45]. These details imply that the Crassula ovata plant has the potential to be a carbon source that is rich in doping elements, such as nitrogen.

Fig. 1 depicts the steps used to prepare P, N-CQDs. The synthesis of CQDs was initially studied using the leaves of the Crassula ovata plant heated by a microwave. The generated material had poor emission and excitation spectra shapes that were not even mirror images of one another, as well as low fluorescence intensity. As mentioned before, the weak fluorescence intensity of CQDs may be enhanced by doping them with different heteroatoms, such as phosphorus and nitrogen. Upon the addition of urea (nitrogen source) to Crassula ovata leaves, the fluorescence intensity only slightly increased. Remarkably, employing both NH4H2PO4 and urea with Crassula ovata leaves resulted in a discernible increase in fluorescence intensity with a satisfactory quantum yield of 35.23 %. This might be because NH4H2PO4 has more hydrogen bonding sites, which could enhance CQD doping in addition to serving as a source of N and P. These findings suggested that the utilization of NH4H2PO4 in the synthesis of CQDs can facilitate P-doping in the CQDs and increase the amount of N that is already doped from urea. Fig. S1 demonstrates that NH4H2PO4 improves fluorescence yield in addition to urea. This could be interpreted by the multi-doping strategies as NH4H2PO4 contains multiple elements, such as nitrogen and phosphorus. The synthesis involved testing different urea and ammonium phosphate doping ratios. The best reaction to BXM and the maximum fluorescence QY were produced at a 2:1 (w/w) ratio of urea and ammonium phosphate, respectively. Changing this ratio led to both reduced quenching efficiency and lower QY of CQDs (Fig. S2).

With the use of several spectroscopy methods, the spectral characteristics and functional groups at the surface of P, N-CQDs were investigated. In the FT/IR spectrum of the P, N-CQDs (Fig. 2 a), a large and strong stretching band at 3358 cm−1 is assigned to (–OH) and (–N–H–). Additionally, the two bands at 1055 and 1407 cm−1 are ascribed to the hydroxyl group's C-OH stretching and –OH bending. There are stretching and vibrational bands at 1641 and 1226 cm−1, and 889 cm−1 respectively, which indicate the presence of the C=N, P=O, and P–O–C. It has been determined that the surface-OH groups of P, N-CQDs can improve their stability, water solubility, and hydrophilicity. The surface and electron density of P, N-CQDs were investigated utilizing a Zeta potential analyzer. The zeta potential of the synthesized P, N-CQDs was − 40 mV, indicating that they possessed negatively charged surfaces due to doping with electron-rich groups (a substantial quantity of oxygen-containing groups as well as N and P-rich groups) and confirming their stability (Fig. 2b). HRTEM was utilized to observe the shape and size of the synthesized P, N-CQDs. In this technique, the sample was placed on a Cu-grid with a carbon covering around 200 meshes, and HRTEM was operated at 200 kV. The P, N-CQDs were observed to be spherical, uniform, and monodispersed with no discernible aggregation. The majority of the particles are dispersed between 1.5 and 3.0 nm, with an average diameter of 2.4 nm, confirming the creation of nano sized particles, as demonstrated in Fig. 2c. Furthermore, X-ray diffraction (XRD) offers important and fundamental information about the material, such as d-spacing, composition, crystal structure, as well as crystal size. Fig. 3 a shows the XRD pattern of the produced P, N-CQDs, with a sharp diffraction peak at a 2θ value of 27°, comparable to the (002) lattice spacing of graphite-type materials, demonstrating the crystalline structure of the carbogenic core. The contents and types of elements in the fabricated P, N-CQDs were detected utilizing energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). Fig. 3b represents EDX analysis of P, N-CQDs, which confirms that the C and O content are 20.92 % and 41.46 %, respectively, indicating that P, N-CQDs are primarily carbon-based. The N and P contents were 30.75 % and 6.87 %, demonstrating that P, N-CQDs are highly doped with N and P. Additionally, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis revealed the effective incorporation of phosphorus and nitrogen into P, N-CQDs. The spectrum exhibited recognizable peaks for C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and P 2p (Fig. 4 ). Different surface functions were indicated by peaks for the C–C/C=C (∼284.5 eV), C–N (∼285.7 eV), C–O (∼286.5 eV), and C=O (∼288.0 eV) groups that were found by high-resolution deconvolution of the C 1s spectra. To prove successful nitrogen doping, the N 1s spectra showed peaks at approximately 399–401 eV. Effective phosphorus incorporation is suggested by the P 2p region's peaks, which correspond to P–O and P–C bonds and are located at about ∼133–134 eV. The O 1s spectra showed distinct peaks at ∼531 eV and ∼532.5 eV, corresponding to carbonyl (C=O) and hydroxyl (C-OH) groups. These oxygen-containing groups enhance the stability and hydrophilicity of P, N-CQDs in aqueous conditions, as well as their fluorescence behavior.

The optical characteristics of the P, N-CQDs were also explored using UV–Vis spectrophotometry and spectrofluorimetry. The P, N-CQDs displayed a maximum UV–Vis absorption peak between 210 and 300 nm, with λmax at roughly 231 nm. Surface states capturing excited-state energy were the cause of this peak, which subsequently resulted in high emissions. The emission spectrum of P, N-CQDs, which shows a redshift in the range of 290–400 nm and no regular pattern as the excitation wavelength increases, confirms that the fluorescence emission is dependent on the excitation of the CQDs (Fig. S3). The high fluorescence intensity of P, N-CQDs was measured at 405 nm after being excited at 320 nm. The quantum yield (QY) of the P, N-CQDs was determined to be 35.23 %, which is higher than the majority of reports for producing CQDs from natural sources. Also, the PY of P, N-CQDs was determined as stated in Section ''2.3.'' and observed to be 28 %.

Generally, the interaction of CQDs with drugs can be explained by a variety of mechanisms, such as the inner filter effect (IFE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), in addition to static and dynamic interactions [26]. IFE is a type of non-irradiation energy conversion, which occurs when analytes in the detecting system absorb the emission and/or excitation radiation of fluorophores [33]. IFE can only be effectively revealed if the UV spectra of analytes overlap with the emission and/or excitation spectra of fluorophores. IFE is divided into two kinds: primary IFF and secondary IFE. Primary IFE is the absorption of excitation light from fluorophores, whereas secondary IFE is the absorption of emission light by absorbers. FRET is a process where the energy is transmitted from the donor molecule's excited state to the acceptor molecule. FRET may occur only if the quencher's absorption spectrum overlaps with the emission peak of the fluorescent molecule, allowing energy to be transferred between both. Additionally, the energy difference between the first electronic excited state and the lowest energy level of the acceptor and donor must be compatible. Static and dynamic quenching are other important mechanisms that can be inferred from the Stern-Volmer equation. Dynamic mechanism involves the analyte (BXM) moving in the excited state and coming into contact with the fluorophore, whereas static mechanism is caused by the formation of the ground state complex [46]. The quenching mechanism is demonstrated to be static rather than dynamic if the quenching rate constant (kq) is higher than the maximum diffusion rate constant, and vice versa [37].

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the addition of BXM significantly reduced the blue fluorescence of P, N-CQDs. The emission intensity of P, N-CQDs was quantitatively quenched by increasing the BXM concentration (0.2, 0.6, 1.0, 3.0, 6.0, 8.0, 10.0, 14.0, 16.0, 18.0 μg/mL). A methodical approach was utilized to determine the possible mechanisms of quenching. Initially, the UV spectra of the studied drug and the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs were analyzed. As presented in Fig. 5b, BXM exhibits a UV-absorption peak that overlaps strongly with the excitation wavelength of P, N-CQDs, indicating the presence of primary IFE. To better clarify this mechanism, Eq. (3) was used to correct the measurements of the CQD's fluorescence intensity in the presence of increasing BXM concentrations.
(3)

2
Fobs and Fcorr denote the observed fluorescence and corrected fluorescence. Aex and Aem represent the absorbance of BXM at the excitation and emission wavelengths. Moreover, the suppressed efficiency (%E) of the corrected and observed fluorescence was measured, as demonstrated in Eq. (4):
(4)
% E =
1 -
F

Where % E is the suppression efficiency, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the blank and Fcorr or Fobs. The correlation between BXM concentrations (μg/mL) and both the corrected and observed fluorescence intensities of P, N-CQDs (%E) (Fig. 5c) proved the occurrence of the IFE mechanism.

Mechanisms of static or dynamic quenching can be studied by the Stern-Volmer equation, as shown in Eq. (5):
(5)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K

Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
Where F0 and F are the P, N-CQDs fluorescence intensities at λex/λem wavelengths of 320 nm and 405 nm before and after BXM addition; ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, kq is the fluorescence quenching constant; τ0 is the average lifetime (10-8 s) [31,42,47]; and [Q] is the molar concentration of BXM. The proposed method was studied at various temperatures (293, 303, and 313 K) to explore the presence of static or dynamic quenching mechanisms. The KSV constant rises with increasing temperature in the dynamic quenching mechanism, but the KSV constant decreases with increasing temperature in the static quenching mechanism [48]. The Ksv values were observed to be 23.9 × 103, 13.1 × 103, and 5.4 × 103 L.mol−1 (Fig. 5d). The Ksv values decrease with increasing temperature, confirming the occurrence of static quenching. Furthermore, the values of Kq were calculated and found to be 23.9 × 1011, 13.1 × 1011, and 5.4 × 1011 L. mol−1. S−1, which further ascertains the static quenching mechanism. To further validate static quenching, the binding constant (Kb) and thermodynamic parameters (ΔH, ΔS) were calculated using the modified Stern-Volmer equation and van’t Hoff analysis. The binding constant (Kb) between P,N-CQDs and BXM was quantified using the following equation:
(6)
L o g [ ( F 0 - F ) / F ] = l o g
K b + n l o g
[ Q ]
The binding constant (Kb) was found to be in the order of 105. This strong binding supports the formation of a ground-state complex.

From the temperature-dependent KSV data, a van’t Hoff plot (lnK vs. 1/T) was constructed, and the enthalpy (ΔH) and entropy (ΔS) changes were calculated from the slope and intercept of the linear plot, respectively using the following equation:
(7)
L n
K = [ - ( Δ H / R )
∗
( 1 / T ) ]
+ ( Δ S / R )

The values of ΔH and ΔS were calculated and found to be −56.48 kJ/mol and −108.47 J/mol. The negative ΔH value indicates an exothermic binding process, and the negative ΔS value suggests reduced entropy, which is consistent with complex formation. Therefore, temperature dependence confirms static quenching mechanism [49]. To summarize, the mechanism of quenching includes both static quenching and IFE.

To achieve the best sensitivity and robustness, the variables that affect quenching efficiency (F0-F) were thoroughly examined. These variables include the P, N-CQDs' volume, the type of diluting solvent, the pH of the buffer, the buffer volume, and the incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the P, N-CQDs' volume
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the volume of P, N-CQDs was investigated over the range of 10.0–60.0 μL. Quenching was most effective at 40.0–60.0 μL. It was observed that the ideal volume for a robust assay is 50.0 μL (Fig. S4a).

Since the diluting solvent can alter the fluorescence spectra's form, location, and intensity, its effects were investigated. The solvents that were examined included deionized water, ethanol, acetonitrile, and methanol. As shown in Fig. S4b, it was observed that deionized water and ethanol generated the greatest signals. However, the overall cost of analysis is raised by the extensive use of pricey organic solvents like ethanol. Therefore, deionized water was selected as the optimum diluting solvent.

The effect of pH on fluorescence quenching was studied because the pH of a solution affects how well CQDs interact with the tested drug and how intensely they fluoresce. Using 0.04 M Britton-Robinson buffer with pH ranges of 3–12, the pH influence on F0-F of CQDs emission intensity by BXM was investigated. According to the findings, which are displayed in Fig. S4c, the observed quenching was pH-dependent and the ideal pH was found to be 6.
After evaluating a variety of buffer volumes from 500 µL to 2000 µL, it was revealed that using 1000 µL of BRB at pH 6 was the optimum volume to obtain the largest quenching effect (Fig. S4d).

The influence of incubation time was examined due to the major effect on fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence signals of P, N-CQDs after adding BXM were monitored for 60 min at 5-minute intervals. As shown in Fig. S4e, the quenching effect was observed to be immediate and constant for at least 60 min. This excludes the necessity for incubation time before measurements, speeding up the suggested procedure and reducing overall analysis time. These are significant advantages of this strategy.

The proposed method was validated according to the guidelines of the International Council on Harmonization (ICH) Q2 R2. These guidelines covered linearity and range, accuracy, precision, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantitation (LOQ), robustness, and selectivity. The emission intensity of the generated P, N-CQDs at the emission wavelength of 405 nm was determined with and without various concentrations of BXM.

After obtaining the emission spectra of CQDs with and without the BXM drug under optimal conditions, the calibration curve was created by plotting the fluorescence difference (F0-F) against final drug concentrations in μg/mL Fig. S5. As shown in Table 1 , a rectilinear calibration curve for concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 18.0 μg/mL, with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9994. The linear regression could be stated by the following equation:
(8)
F 0
- F
= 16.21
C
BXM
+ 57.157
F0 and F: The fluorescence intensity of P, N-CQDs in the absence and presence of the BXM drug.
CBXM represents the baloxavir marboxil concentration in μg/mL.

The accuracy of the developed method was evaluated in triplicate runs with various concentrations covering the BXM linearity range, and mean percentage recoveries were calculated (Table S1). High recovery percentages (98.02–101.2 %) were noted, confirming the high accuracy of the method. Intra-day precision was attained by choosing three distinct BXM concentrations within the range and doing a replication assay three times on the same day. As shown in Table S2, the resulting tiny % RSD value verified the intra-day precision. Intermediate precision (inter-day) was verified by repeating the assay of specified concentrations of BXM in pure form across three days. The low % RSD confirmed the high inter-day precision of the proposed approach (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ)
ce:section.ce:para: LOQ and LOD could be measured using the following mathematical formulas: LOQ = 10x/b, LOD = 3.3x/b, where x is the standard deviation of the blank and b is the slope. The results in Table 1 confirm that the proposed method has an acceptable level of sensitivity.

To test the robustness of the developed method, a deliberate change in the parameters was made, such as the excitation wavelength (±5 nm), the volume of P, N-CQDs (±5 µL), the pH of the buffer (±5), and the volume of buffer (±5 µL). The performance of the suggested approach and the system suitability factors were shown to be unaffected by slight changes in these variables. Table S3 shows that acceptable relative standard deviation (RSD%) values were obtained, demonstrating procedure consistency over time.

The selectivity of the proposed method is a crucial parameter for assessing if P, N-CQDs can be utilized to determine BXM in different samples. The selectivity of produced P, N-CQDs to BXM is first ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE), which does not occur with all medications. Secondly, not all medications or unusual substances might interfere with the study because all measurements were made at a wavelength of around 400 nm. Lastly, the probe's fluorescence intensity was examined in the presence of lactose, mannitol, dextrin, maltose, citric acid, carboxy methyl cellulose, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Pb2+, and Ca2+ at concentrations 100 times greater than BXM. As seen in Fig. 6 , the quenching value (F0-F) only rose noticeably in the presence of BXM, suggesting a force interaction between BXM and P, N-CQDs in addition to high selectivity and anti-interference capability. The probe's selectivity was also confirmed when even large concentrations of different metals did not provide noticeable changes. The anti-interference potential of P, N-CQDs against popular co-administered medications, including sofosbuvir, ribavirin, and aspirin, was investigated. Greater tolerance limit values for sofosbuvir (100 μg/mL), ribavirin (100.0 μg/mL), and aspirin (10.0 μg/mL) were found, suggesting the fluorescent probe's selectivity for BXM. Although Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions were found to quench the fluorescence of the P, N-CQDs at high concentrations, their levels were adjusted to lower concentrations, under which no appreciable interference was observed ( Fig. 6 ). Moreover, these metal ions are unlikely to coexist with BXM in common pharmaceutical formulations, reducing the probability of interference in different applications. The tolerance limits of these ions were calculated as the concentration that results in 2 % relative error [43], and the tolerance limit values for Fe3+ and Hg2+ were found to be 2.0 μg/mL and 3.0 μg/mL, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of BXM in dosage forms
ce:section.ce:para: The developed P, N-CQDs fluorescent probe has been employed for the analysis of BXM in commercially available pharmaceutical dosage form (Xofluza® tablets) using five various concentrations of BXM. The % recoveries and SD value were calculated by using the previous regression equation of the calibration curve. As demonstrated in Table 2 , the high % recoveries (99.64–101.98 %) with low SD value (±1.02) proved the ability of the prepared P, N-CQDs to determine BXM concentrations in pharmaceutical dosage forms without any interferences from co-administered drugs, metals, as well as different additives or excipients. According to the student's t-test and variance ratio F-test values shown in Table S4, the results obtained by the developed method for BXM determination in Xofluza® tablets showed an excellent agreement with the data obtained using the reported fluorimetric method [5]. The proposed method and the comparative method did not differ significantly in accuracy and precision since the calculated values did not exceed the tabulated ones.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of BXM in spiked human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: The reported maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) of BXM (206.9 ng/mL) [50] is within the linearity range of the studied approach. The developed approach successfully determines BXM in the human plasma with high sensitivity and selectivity, without being influenced by the plasma components. As shown in Table 2, the mean % recovery and standard deviation of BXM in human plasma were calculated and found to be 100.23 % ± 1.06, indicating the good bioanalytical applicability of the developed method. These results for BXM determination in spiked human plasma were also compared to those obtained by the reported method [5]. It was shown that there is no significant difference between the two methods after calculating the t-value and F-value (Table S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness assessment of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: Green chemistry plays a key role in both managing pollution levels and solving environmental issues worldwide. As a result, it becomes imperative to develop and assess green analytical methods that rely on energy efficiency, the use of renewable green reagents, and the avoidance of hazardous chemicals. Various tools, including the analytical Eco-scale [51,52], the Complex Modified Green Analytical Procedure Index (ComplexMoGAPI) [53], and the Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) [54], have been applied to evaluate the analytical methods' greenness. The analytical Eco-scale was used to assess the approach's ecology [51,52]. To determine the method's level of greenness, a numerical score is calculated, with 100 representing the ideal green approach. Each negative environmental effect (such as the utilization of hazardous chemicals, waste production, as well as extreme energy usage) is deducted from the total of 100 points using the formula Analytical Eco-Scale = 100 − total penalty points. The greater the value, the greater ecologically friendly the procedure is. In the proposed approach, water was utilized instead of organic solvents, and no sample was exposed to more than 0.1 kWh of power-hungry or heating processes. With a score of 88 on the Eco-scale, the recommended approach was deemed an excellent green method (Table 3 ). Recently, a new ComplexMoGAPI software was used to assess the developed method's greenness [53]. Mansour et al. published a report on the ComplexMoGAPI tool, which combines the graphical design of ComplexGAPI with accurate total scores. It is specifically made to evaluate the greenness of analytical procedures that use materials that have been synthesized before the analytical step, like the PN-CDs used in the suggested procedure. The degree of greenness in the synthesis step is represented by the lower hexagon in the pictogram in Fig. S6a. It includes workup, purification, safety and health risks, and condition assessment. The top five pentagons illustrate the green aspects of the analytical process that involve reagents, processes, and equipment. They consider several factors, including quantity, health effects, solvent and reagent safety, energy consumption, waste production, and method nature. Green, yellow, and red denote low, medium, and high environmental risks, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6a, Table S5, the developed method accomplished a ComplexMoGAPI score of 85, indicating its excellent greenness. The AGREE tool was also utilized to provide a fair assessment of the environmental effects of the proposed technology. The twelve Green Analytical Chemistry (GAC) principles are represented by the twelve segments of the clock-shaped graph of AGREE. Each segment is colored (red, yellow, and green) to indicate a different attitude depending on how ecologically friendly the analytical procedure is. The total evaluation value is shown on a graph with a scale from 0 to 1. The suggested method's greenness is demonstrated by the score of 0.73, as seen in Fig. S6b, Table S6. Analysis of the factors contributing to this greenness characteristic showed that the developed approach has a lot of green values that support the application of the sustainability concept in analytical chemistry labs. First, employing clean MW radiation to create plant-based P, N-CQDs is a straightforward, energy-efficient, and quick manner. Second, the use of tricolor crassula leaves as a natural carbon source to obtain the P, N-CQDs improved the suggested method's greenness. Third, the developed method's cost-efficiency makes it suitable for widespread use in low-income labs. Fourth, the developed method ensures environmental and human safety. Fifth, organic solvents and dangerous compounds are excluded. The developed approach is more economical, uses less energy, and takes less time than the previous hydrothermal methods.

The efficiency of the P, N-CQDs as a fluorescence sensor has been compared to the published analytical methods for determining BXM. As shown in Table 4 , the mentioned approaches are energy and time-consuming. Other methods involved the use of large amounts of hazardous compounds or organic solvents, including methanol, formic acid, acetonitrile, and acetic acid. However, the proposed approach avoids using such chemicals and extreme circumstances. The developed approach is more sensitive than the published UPLC method, which depended on forced degradation and using high amounts of toxic solvents such as methanol [4]. It also has higher sensitivity than the reported spectrofluorimetric method, which relied on a derivatization reaction with acetoxymercuric reagent [5]. Although the reported spectrofluorimetric approach had higher sensitivity than the current method, it required many stages, such as heating at high temperatures, a long synthesis period, and extraction of the synthetic product [6]. Furthermore, the reported LC-MS methods necessitate a highly skilled individual as well as a complex and expensive instrument [7–9]. In contrast, our developed method is a highly fast and green procedure since the interaction between BXM and P, N-CQDs occurs immediately, and no toxic chemicals are used during the operation. The developed method's novelty and analytical benefits were further emphasized by a comparison with recent CQD-based fluorescence methods that have been described for the determination of other antiviral drugs. For instance, molnupiravir was quantified using microwave-assisted green-synthesized CQDs made from garlic peels with a linear range of 0.5–30.0 μg/mL and a detection limit of 0.19 μg/mL [55]. CQDs made from eggshell waste were also used in another investigation to determine molnupiravir with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.8 μg/mL [56]. Moreover, CQDs derived from yellow lupin peel waste were utilized for the detection of nirmatrelvir through a quenching mechanism, with acceptable selectivity and sensitivity in pharmaceutical dosage forms [57]. The synthesis of these CQDs was straightforward, and their sensitivity was satisfactory. Compared to these approaches, the synthesized P, N-CQDs were produced in a single-step microwave-assisted method that did not require surface passivation or organic modifiers. The designed probe has a wide linear range of 0.2–18.0 µg/mL and a lower detection limit of 0.04 µg/mL for determining BXM, indicating the method's excellent sensitivity. The approach was very selective even in the presence of interfering ions, co-administered drugs, and excipients. These findings demonstrate the advantages of the suggested nanosensor in terms of selectivity, simplicity, and sensitivity, making it a dependable and environmentally friendly alternative for antiviral drug analysis in different pharmaceutical dosage forms and biological samples.

A novel, efficient, and quick method was developed for the synthesis of CQDs using Tricolor Crassula leaves as natural carbon sources with urea and ammonium phosphate monobasic as nitrogen and phosphorus sources, respectively. This combination was microwave-treated for only 80 s without the use of harsh chemicals or organic solvents. The produced P, N-CQDs are distinguished by their hydrophilicity and strong fluorescence quantum yield of 35.23 %. It is an easy, rapid, single-pot, inexpensive, green, and promising synthesis method. Thus, the produced P, N-CQDs were used to ensure the quality of BXM in pharmaceutical dosage forms. Due to its exceptional performance and selectivity for BXM detection, the proposed approach was used to evaluate BXM in human plasma with a good %recovery and low standard deviation of 100.23 ± 1.06. Additionally, the novel analytical method showed remarkable environmental friendliness by avoiding the use of organic solvents, hazardous chemicals, high temperatures, or unnecessary time consumption.
